# Pitfalls Research

**Domain:** Flutter codebase cleanup — Riverpod / Freezed / Drift + SQLCipher / flutter_localizations, strict behavior preservation
**Researched:** 2026-04-25
**Confidence:** HIGH (findings grounded in actual codebase state from `.planning/codebase/` map plus direct file inspection)

---

## Critical Pitfalls

### Pitfall 1: Stale Generated Files After Any Refactor Touch

**What goes wrong:**
A hand-written file annotated with `@riverpod`, `@freezed`, `@DriftDatabase`, or `@GenerateMocks` is edited — a class renamed, a constructor parameter added, a provider function signature changed — but `build_runner` is not re-run. The corresponding `.g.dart` or `.freezed.dart` still references the old name. `flutter analyze` reports 0 errors only because `analysis_options.yaml` excludes generated files (`**/*.g.dart`, `**/*.freezed.dart`). The project compiles because the *old* generated code still typechecks against the stale source. The mismatch surfaces at runtime as a `NoSuchMethodError` or silent wrong behavior.

**Why it happens:**
The `analysis_options.yaml` in this project correctly excludes generated files from analysis. This is standard and correct — but it means the analyzer cannot catch a hand-written file that no longer matches its generated counterpart. Developers who edit a provider function and run `flutter analyze` get "0 issues" and conclude they are done.

**How to avoid:**
- Run `flutter pub run build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs` after every edit to a `@riverpod`, `@freezed`, `@DriftDatabase`, or `@GenerateMocks`-annotated file, and after every git merge/rebase/pull (per `CLAUDE.md`).
- Add a CI check: `flutter pub run build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs && git diff --exit-code lib/` — fails the build if any generated file changed, indicating the committed generated files were stale.
- During refactor phases, run build_runner in watch mode: `flutter pub run build_runner watch`.
- **After deleting a provider function**, check that its `.g.dart` part no longer references it. `--delete-conflicting-outputs` handles this, but only when re-run.

**Warning signs:**
- `part 'foo.g.dart';` file referenced by a hand-written file does not match its source.
- `NoSuchMethodError` in provider graph at startup.
- `flutter test` passes but `flutter run` crashes.
- Mock files (committed `*.mocks.dart`) reference interface methods that no longer exist after renaming.

**Phase to address:**
Every phase. Enforce via pre-commit gate: `build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs && git diff --exit-code lib/`.

---

### Pitfall 2: Committed Mockito `.mocks.dart` Files Become Stale Silently

**What goes wrong:**
This project has 14 committed `*.mocks.dart` files generated by Mockito (`@GenerateMocks([...])`). When a repository interface is refactored (method added, parameter renamed, return type changed), the committed mock file still contains the old signatures. Tests that use the stale mock continue to compile and even pass — because the mock returns whatever `thenAnswer` specifies, not what the real implementation returns. Coverage numbers look good. Regressions are invisible until integration testing catches a shape mismatch.

**Why it happens:**
Mockito-generated mocks are committed to version control in this project (confirmed: 14 files). They are only regenerated when `build_runner` is run. A refactor that changes `TransactionRepository.findById` signature does not automatically invalidate the committed `MockTransactionRepository`. If `build_runner` is not run, old mock compiles fine, tests pass, bug ships.

**How to avoid:**
- Add `*.mocks.dart` to `.gitignore` and regenerate as part of CI (preferred for long-term health). This project's `TESTING.md` notes the 14 committed mocks as a "Low" priority concern — the cleanup is the time to either commit to regenerating on CI or keep them committed with a strict policy of always regenerating after interface changes.
- If keeping committed: treat committed mock files as requiring an update whenever any mocked interface changes. Use a script: `grep -r "@GenerateMocks" test/ | sort | uniq` to enumerate all mocked types; check each against its interface after refactor.
- Prefer Mocktail over Mockito for new test files (Mocktail requires no code gen; `TESTING.md` notes it as preferred for newer infrastructure tests).

**Warning signs:**
- A test passes but the mock's stub is for a method that was renamed — the renamed method is never actually called.
- `verify(mock.oldMethodName()).called(1)` passes because the mock still has the old name.
- Running `build_runner build` after a refactor generates a mock file that differs from the committed one.

**Phase to address:**
Phase covering dead-code and redundancy elimination (wherever interfaces are modified). Run `build_runner build` as the first action in any test suite before relying on results.

---

### Pitfall 3: Two Classes Named `CategoryService` with Different Contracts

**What goes wrong:**
The codebase has two distinct classes both named `CategoryService`:
- `lib/application/accounting/category_service.dart` — concrete class with business logic, depends on repositories.
- `lib/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart` — `abstract final class` with static locale maps (735 lines), no dependencies.

These are consumed by different call sites (infrastructure one imported by `home_screen.dart`, `transaction_form_screen.dart`, `category_display_utils.dart`; application one imported by `use_case_providers.dart`). During refactor, if an import path is corrected or unified without understanding both exist, code silently starts using the wrong one. The infrastructure one returns locale-map strings; the application one queries repositories. Substituting one for the other passes type-checking (they share method names like `resolveLedgerType` at the surface) but returns fundamentally different data.

**Why it happens:**
Fast greenfield growth created parallel implementations. The infrastructure one predates the application one. Both serve legitimate but overlapping purposes: one handles localization of category names (static), the other handles ledger-type classification (async, database-backed). The naming collision is a ticking clock.

**How to avoid:**
- Before touching any `CategoryService` import, confirm which of the two files is meant. Both exist and both compile.
- As part of the redundant-code phase: rename `lib/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart` to `CategoryLocaleService` or `CategoryDisplayService` to eliminate the namespace collision. Update all import sites.
- Add a `flutter analyze` custom rule or grep CI check: `grep -r "category_service.dart" lib/ | grep -v "# only one allowed"` — should resolve to exactly one import path per feature, not two.

**Warning signs:**
- `import '…/application/accounting/category_service.dart'` and `import '…/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart'` both appear in the same file.
- Refactored code passes `flutter analyze` but returns empty/wrong display strings for category names.

**Phase to address:**
Redundant-code phase. Must be resolved before any provider consolidation that touches `categoryServiceProvider`.

---

### Pitfall 4: `appDatabaseProvider` Override Missing in Test ProviderScope

**What goes wrong:**
`appDatabaseProvider` is defined in `lib/infrastructure/security/providers.dart` as a placeholder that throws `UnimplementedError`. The override is only applied in `lib/main.dart` via `ProviderContainer(overrides: [...])`. Any widget test or provider test that constructs its own `ProviderScope(...)` without explicitly overriding `appDatabaseProvider` will throw at runtime the moment any provider in the graph watches `appDatabaseProvider` — directly or transitively. This is a silent pitfall because most existing tests mock at the repository level and never hit `appDatabaseProvider` directly. But if the refactor moves any provider to watch `appDatabaseProvider` directly (e.g., consolidating DAOs), untouched tests that worked before will suddenly fail with `UnimplementedError`.

**Why it happens:**
The placeholder-plus-override pattern is a valid Riverpod pattern for app initialization. The problem is that it relies on every test environment replicating the override. If a test was written against a mocked repository (never touching `appDatabaseProvider`), the overlay is unnecessary. But if refactoring makes a provider's dependency graph now include `appDatabaseProvider`, the test breaks with a confusing `UnimplementedError` that gives no indication of what override is missing.

**How to avoid:**
- In every `testWidgets` or provider test that constructs `ProviderScope(overrides: [...])`, add `appDatabaseProvider.overrideWithValue(AppDatabase.forTesting())` as a default precaution.
- Create a shared test helper `createTestProviderScope({required List<Override> overrides})` that always includes the `appDatabaseProvider` override, so it's impossible to forget.
- Add an integration test that verifies the override is wired in `main.dart` (as noted in `CONCERNS.md`).

**Warning signs:**
- Test fails with `UnimplementedError: appDatabaseProvider must be overridden`.
- Test was passing before a provider refactor and now fails without any change to the test file itself.

**Phase to address:**
Any phase touching the `lib/infrastructure/security/providers.dart` or reorganizing the provider dependency graph. Must be caught before coverage gates are evaluated.

---

### Pitfall 5: Drift Schema Refactor Without Migration Ladder Update

**What goes wrong:**
If a table definition in `lib/data/tables/` is modified during cleanup (e.g., a column renamed, an index added, a table split) and `schemaVersion` in `lib/data/app_database.dart` is not incremented with a corresponding migration step, Drift will silently skip the migration on existing databases. The app installs with a new schema definition but reads data from an old schema — queries against the new column name fail with `SqliteException` on upgraded devices while working fine on fresh installs. This is not a new risk unique to cleanup, but cleanup that touches table definitions for index additions (specifically: adding `TableIndex` to `audit_logs_table.dart`, `user_profiles_table.dart`, `category_ledger_configs_table.dart` as noted in `CONCERNS.md`) must follow the migration ladder.

**Why it happens:**
Adding indices in Drift via `customIndices` does **not** automatically require a migration if the table itself has not changed — but Drift's migration checking is at the schema level. If the migration ladder is not updated to include an `addIndex` or `createIndex` step, upgraded devices will not have the index. The app works (no crash) but has degraded query performance, and the index is silently absent.

**How to avoid:**
- Any addition of `customIndices` to a table requires: (1) bump `schemaVersion`, (2) add a migration step using `createIndex` or `customStatement('CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS ...')`.
- Never add columns or indices to tables in `lib/data/tables/` without a corresponding migration step in `lib/data/app_database.dart`.
- Run `AppDatabase.forTesting()` in a test that opens a v13 schema and runs migrations to v15 (new version), then asserts the expected indices exist via `PRAGMA index_list(table_name)`.
- The existing migration from v3→v14 uses string interpolation for SQL (`'${cat.id}'`, `'${cat.name}'`) — this is a documented risk (`CONCERNS.md`). During cleanup, do NOT copy this pattern into new migration steps. Use parameterized queries: `customStatement('...VALUES (?, ?)', [cat.id, cat.name])`.

**Warning signs:**
- Fresh install works; upgrade from a previous version produces `SqliteException` with column-not-found or no-such-index.
- `flutter test` passes (in-memory DB always starts fresh at current schema) but physical device upgrade fails.
- A migration step exists in the code but a coverage-only test runs `AppDatabase.forTesting()` without ever triggering the migration path.

**Phase to address:**
Any phase touching `lib/data/tables/` files. The index-addition cleanup specifically (three tables cited in `CONCERNS.md`).

---

### Pitfall 6: Deleting `ResolveLedgerTypeService` Before All Call Sites Migrate

**What goes wrong:**
`ResolveLedgerTypeService` is marked `@Deprecated('Use CategoryService instead')` and retained with three `// ignore: deprecated_member_use_from_same_package` suppressions in `use_case_providers.dart`. The service still has:
- A dedicated test file: `test/unit/application/dual_ledger/resolve_ledger_type_service_test.dart`
- Mock definitions in two test `*.mocks.dart` files
- Usage in `resolveLedgerTypeServiceProvider` in the provider graph

Deleting the service file before (a) verifying every call site migrates to `CategoryService.resolveLedgerType`, (b) deleting the provider from `use_case_providers.dart` and its generated `.g.dart`, (c) regenerating mocks, and (d) updating or deleting the test — will produce a cascade of compile errors. The danger is that cascade errors from one deletion are fixed hastily, causing another deletion to be incomplete.

**Why it happens:**
Deprecated code creates a "false floor" — developers treat the `@Deprecated` annotation as proof the code is safe to delete. But deletion must be complete: source file, provider, generated code, mocks, tests. Missing any one leaves a compile error or a dangling provider reference.

**How to avoid:**
Follow a deletion checklist for each deprecated item:
1. Find all import sites: `grep -r "resolve_ledger_type_service" lib/ test/`
2. Migrate each import site to the replacement
3. Delete the source file
4. Delete the provider from `use_case_providers.dart`
5. Run `build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs`
6. Delete or update the dedicated test file
7. Regenerate mocks for any test that had `MockResolveLedgerTypeService`
8. Run `flutter analyze` — must be 0 issues
9. Run `flutter test` — must pass

**Warning signs:**
- Deleted source file but provider still references it — compile error.
- Provider deleted but test mock still imports the type — compile error after mock regeneration.
- `flutter analyze` shows 0 issues but `flutter test` fails because a mock is stale.

**Phase to address:**
Dead-code elimination phase. Deprecated code deletion is a prerequisite for provider hygiene consolidation.

---

### Pitfall 7: ARB Key Deletion Without Dynamic Usage Audit

**What goes wrong:**
The EN ARB file has 197 more key entries than JA and ZH (`app_en.arb` has ~337 keys; `app_ja.arb` and `app_zh.arb` have ~239 keys each — counting non-`@` entries). During dead-code cleanup, keys absent from all three non-generated source files might appear "orphaned" and be deleted. But several keys like `ocrScan`, `ocrScanTitle`, `ocrHint` exist in the ARB files and are generated into `lib/generated/` but have no current call site in `lib/` (the OCR screen is not implemented). These are not dead — they are placeholders for upcoming features (MOD-005 OCR). Deleting them requires updating all three ARB files and regenerating `flutter gen-l10n`.

Additionally, `lib/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart` maintains parallel `_ja`, `_zh`, `_en` static maps that duplicate ARB content. These static maps are used via dynamic key lookup (`CategoryService.getName(id, locale)`), bypassing the generated `S` class. A grep for `S.of(context).categoryFoo` will find 0 references to category name keys — but the keys are consumed indirectly through the static map. Deleting category-related ARB keys because "nothing references them statically" would be wrong.

**How to avoid:**
- Before deleting any ARB key, audit whether it exists in the static locale maps in `lib/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart` (735 lines). If a category name ARB key is "unused" statically but appears in the static map, the key is still live.
- Mark OCR-placeholder keys (ocrScan, ocrScanTitle, ocrHint, etc.) as intentional stubs with a `"@ocrScan": { "description": "OCR placeholder — do not delete, MOD-005" }` comment.
- Add a build-time consistency check: all keys in `_ja`, `_zh`, `_en` static maps that correspond to ARB keys should match the ARB content. Drift between the two is already flagged as a `CONCERNS.md` risk.
- When deleting an ARB key, verify it is absent from all three locale files, regenerate `flutter gen-l10n`, run `flutter analyze`, confirm no generated Dart references break.

**Warning signs:**
- Deleting a key from `app_en.arb` but not `app_ja.arb`/`app_zh.arb` will cause `flutter gen-l10n` to fail (locale mismatch).
- A key exists in 1 of 3 ARB files — `flutter gen-l10n` will either error or generate inconsistent outputs depending on whether the key is marked `"@": { "optional": true }`.
- Tests pass (mocked locale) but device shows blank category names.

**Phase to address:**
Dead-code phase, specifically i18n cleanup. Must audit static locale maps before any ARB key deletion.

---

## Moderate Pitfalls

### Pitfall 8: Riverpod `autoDispose` vs `keepAlive` Lifecycle After Provider Consolidation

**What goes wrong:**
Several providers are annotated `@Riverpod(keepAlive: true)` because they manage resources that must survive screen navigation: `SyncEngine` (timers, WebSocket), `TransactionChangeTracker`, `MerchantDatabase` (in-memory dataset), `activeGroupProvider`. If a provider consolidation during the hygiene phase moves a `keepAlive: true` provider definition to a different file, or if a duplicate provider definition is deleted and the surviving one does not have `keepAlive: true`, the resource will be garbage-collected when no widget is watching it. The `SyncEngine` losing its provider keepalive causes sync timers to cancel silently. The `TransactionChangeTracker` losing keepalive causes dedup state to reset mid-session.

**Why it happens:**
Provider consolidation focuses on eliminating duplicate *definitions*. The choice of which definition survives is often made by similarity of code, not by whether the `keepAlive` annotation is on the correct one. Two definitions of the "same" provider may differ exactly in their `keepAlive` status.

**How to avoid:**
- For every provider involved in a consolidation, capture the current `keepAlive` value before merging. The surviving definition must preserve the `keepAlive: true` if *any* of the originals had it.
- Write tests that verify lifecycle: create a `ProviderContainer`, read the provider, dispose all listeners, and assert the provider's state is still alive (for `keepAlive` providers) or has been disposed (for `autoDispose` ones).
- Known `keepAlive: true` providers to preserve: `syncEngineProvider`, `transactionChangeTrackerProvider`, `merchantDatabaseProvider` (in `voice_providers.dart`), `activeGroupProvider`, `activeGroupMembersProvider`, `ledgerProvider` (in `ledger_providers.dart`).

**Warning signs:**
- Sync stops working silently mid-session after navigating away from the sync screen.
- A `keepAlive` provider starts showing `AsyncLoading` after every route change (it is being re-created because autoDispose now fires).
- Tests that verify sync behavior fail intermittently (race between dispose and reconnect).

**Phase to address:**
Riverpod hygiene phase. Lifecycle audit should be a pre-condition for any provider consolidation.

---

### Pitfall 9: `ref.read` vs `ref.watch` Confusion During Provider Refactor

**What goes wrong:**
The codebase uses `ref.read` correctly for event handlers (button presses, one-shot init calls) and `ref.watch` correctly for reactive providers. During refactor, moving a `ref.read` call into a provider body (instead of an event handler) creates a non-reactive dependency — the provider will not rebuild when the read dependency changes. Conversely, moving a `ref.watch` call into a `Notifier.build` teardown or disposal code creates a subscription after the provider is disposed, causing `ProviderException: Tried to use a provider after it was disposed`.

**How to avoid:**
- `ref.read` is only valid in: event handlers (`onPressed`, `onTap`), `Notifier` methods (not `build`), and one-shot initialization code outside `build`.
- `ref.watch` is only valid in: provider `build` methods, widget `build` methods.
- During any provider move or consolidation, verify which of `read`/`watch` is used and whether the new location is appropriate.
- `flutter analyze` with `riverpod_lint: ^2.6.4` (already in `dev_dependencies`) will catch `ref.read` inside `build` and `ref.watch` inside callbacks — this linter is the primary prevention tool.

**Warning signs:**
- A provider does not update when its dependency changes (using `ref.read` in provider body).
- `ProviderException: Tried to use a provider after it was disposed` in logs after navigation.
- `riverpod_lint` warnings appear in `flutter analyze` output after provider refactor.

**Phase to address:**
Riverpod hygiene phase. Run `flutter analyze` with `riverpod_lint` enabled after every provider change.

---

### Pitfall 10: Behavior-Preservation False Positive — Widget Refactors Silently Drop `FontFeature.tabularFigures()`

**What goes wrong:**
`AppTextStyles.amountLarge`, `amountMedium`, `amountSmall` (in `lib/core/theme/app_text_styles.dart`) include `FontFeature.tabularFigures()` for numeric column alignment. There are 11 confirmed usages in non-generated source. However, 164 `TextStyle(` instances exist across the codebase — most are for non-monetary text, but any one of them used for amounts that bypasses the `AppTextStyles` constants will silently render amounts without tabular figures. During cleanup of the theme system (the "TODO: Remove after all screens are migrated to Wa-Modern" items), if a developer replaces `AppTextStyles.amountLarge` with a raw `TextStyle(fontSize: 24, fontWeight: FontWeight.bold)` to shed a legacy alias, the `FontFeature.tabularFigures()` is dropped. The UI "looks" correct — amounts display — but column alignment breaks when digits have different widths (e.g., "1" vs "8"). This is invisible in unit tests.

**How to avoid:**
- Treat any refactor of `AppTextStyles.amount*` constants as requiring a widget test with `find.byWidgetPredicate` that verifies `FontFeature.tabularFigures()` is in the `TextStyle.fontFeatures` list.
- When migrating legacy theme tokens, explicitly check that `amountLarge/Medium/Small` retain `fontFeatures: [FontFeature.tabularFigures()]`.
- Add a comment to each amount-display `Text` widget: `// Amount: must use AppTextStyles.amount* — see CLAUDE.md`.

**Warning signs:**
- A `Text` widget that displays monetary values uses a raw `TextStyle(...)` without `fontFeatures`.
- A theme migration commit changes `AppTextStyles.amountLarge` without a test verifying `fontFeatures`.
- Digit columns in the transaction list lose alignment after a Wa-Modern theme migration.

**Phase to address:**
Theme cleanup / dead-code phase (the Wa-Modern migration TODOs). Also relevant for any widget refactors touching monetary displays.

---

### Pitfall 11: Deleting "Dead" Code That Is Alive via the Static Locale Map

**What goes wrong:**
`lib/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart` (the abstract final class, 735 lines) contains three static maps (`_ja`, `_zh`, `_en`) that replicate category name translations. These maps provide dynamic key lookup: `CategoryService.getName(categoryId, locale)`. Nothing in the codebase calls `S.of(context).categoryFoodLabel` — the category names are accessed through this static map, not through the generated `S` class. During dead-code elimination, a tool that scans for unused symbols and does not understand the static-map indirection will flag many `_ja` / `_zh` / `_en` map entries as "unreachable" because no code references them by name — they are accessed via runtime string key lookup. Deleting these entries breaks category name display for all non-Japanese locales.

**Why it happens:**
Static analysis cannot follow dynamic key dispatch (`map[dynamicKey]`). Dart's `dart analyze` and most dead-code linters will not flag a `Map<String, String>` entry as unused even if it is only accessed dynamically — but an AI-agent semantic scan might incorrectly conclude that entries never referenced by literal key are dead.

**How to avoid:**
- Treat the entire `_ja`, `_zh`, `_en` maps in `lib/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart` as a single unit. They are dead-code-safe only if the entire `CategoryService.getName` function is dead.
- During the AI-agent semantic scan, explicitly instruct the agent: "Do not flag static map entries as dead code unless the containing map variable is itself unreachable."
- Add a test that exercises `CategoryService.getName` for a representative sample of category IDs in each locale and asserts the returned string is non-null and non-empty.

**Warning signs:**
- AI-agent audit flags `_zh` map entries as "never referenced."
- Category names display as empty strings or category IDs on zh/en locales.

**Phase to address:**
Dead-code elimination phase. Include `category_service.dart` static maps in the explicit "do not delete" allowlist for the audit.

---

### Pitfall 12: Provider Duplication "Fix" That Changes Scope or Lifetime

**What goes wrong:**
Multiple `repository_providers.dart` files exist across features (`accounting`, `analytics`, `family_sync`, `settings`). Each defines its own set of repository providers. A consolidation that moves all repository providers into a single shared file changes the *implicit scope* of each provider. Providers defined with `@riverpod` (no explicit `keepAlive`) are `autoDispose` by default. If a repository provider was previously defined in `features/accounting/presentation/providers/repository_providers.dart` and consumed only within the accounting feature, its autoDispose lifecycle was naturally bounded to when accounting screens are active. Moving it to a global shared file does not change the `autoDispose` behavior — but it makes the provider accessible from features that previously could not reach it, potentially creating unexpected cross-feature provider dependencies that violate the "Thin Feature" rule.

**How to avoid:**
- Provider consolidation is not the same as provider centralization. Each feature's `repository_providers.dart` should remain in its feature folder but be deduplicated within that feature.
- The CLAUDE.md rule is "ONE `repository_providers.dart` per feature" — not one global file for all. Enforce this during hygiene.
- If the audit finds a repository provider defined in two different feature `repository_providers.dart` files, the fix is to move the provider to `lib/data/repositories/` (the shared data layer) and have both features import from there — not to merge the feature files.

**Warning signs:**
- After consolidation, a widget from `features/analytics` can now watch `features/accounting`'s `transactionRepositoryProvider` without going through the application layer — a layer violation.
- A provider that was `autoDispose` (auto-disposed when no watchers) is now being kept alive because a new watcher in a different feature holds a reference.

**Phase to address:**
Riverpod hygiene phase. Apply the "one `repository_providers.dart` per feature" rule as a scope-preservation invariant.

---

### Pitfall 13: Test Coverage Gaming Against the 80% Gate

**What goes wrong:**
The 80% coverage gate on touched files is measured by `flutter test --coverage` generating `coverage/lcov.info`. Line coverage in Dart/Flutter counts whether a statement was *executed*, not whether the assertion verified the correct output. A test that calls `await useCase.execute(param)` without any `expect(...)` covers every line it hits. During cleanup, if a phase adds tests purely to reach the 80% threshold on newly touched files, the tests may cover lines without verifying behavior — hitting every line of a migration step without asserting the database state after migration, for example.

**How to avoid:**
- Require every test to assert at least one observable output: `expect(result.isSuccess, isTrue)`, `verify(mock.method()).called(1)`, `expect(widget, findsOneWidget)`.
- Measure branch coverage in addition to line coverage where possible. For Dart, `lcov.info` includes branch data when tests exercise both `if`/`else` arms.
- Code review of new test files: any test without an `expect()` or `verify()` statement is a test that contributes to line coverage without verifying behavior.
- For database repository tests, always use `AppDatabase.forTesting()` (real in-memory SQLite) rather than mocking the DAO — mocking a DAO covers the repository's lines without exercising SQL.

**Warning signs:**
- A test file has `test('...', () async { await x.execute(); });` with no assertions.
- Coverage goes up but `flutter analyze` shows the covered method always returns `Result.success()` regardless of input.
- A migration test exercises `MigrationStrategy.onCreate` but never queries the database to verify the schema.

**Phase to address:**
All phases. Enforce in PR review: every test method must contain at least one `expect()` or `verify()`.

---

### Pitfall 14: Severity Misclassification — "Minor" Issues That Are Load-Bearing

**What goes wrong:**
The cleanup initiative sequences work by severity: CRITICAL → HIGH → MEDIUM → LOW. Misclassification of a critical issue as "medium" means it stays unaddressed until a late phase, by which time other fixes may have been layered on top of the broken foundation. Concrete risks in this codebase:

- `appDatabaseProvider` throwing `UnimplementedError` (classified as a placeholder/low concern in `CONCERNS.md`) — but this is a runtime crash for any test or code path that fails to set up the override. If any MEDIUM-phase cleanup exposes a new code path that reaches this provider without the override, the app crashes.
- The `recoverFromSeed()` key-overwrite bug (classified HIGH in `CONCERNS.md`) — this is not directly a cleanup concern but if the cleanup phase adds tests to `KeyRepositoryImpl`, the existing bug may be inadvertently triggered, destroying keys in test environments that use real `flutter_secure_storage`.
- The database migration string interpolation (SQL injection risk on category names with apostrophes) — classified as MEDIUM fragility in `CONCERNS.md`. If the cleanup adds new system categories to `DefaultCategories.all` that contain apostrophes (e.g., `D'arc`), the existing v14 migration SQL breaks.

**How to avoid:**
- During the audit phase, explicitly challenge the severity classification of any issue that: (a) can crash the app at runtime, (b) affects data integrity, or (c) blocks migration. These are CRITICAL regardless of how "contained" they appear.
- Before starting each phase, run a regression test on all lower-severity issues that the current phase's changes might activate.

**Warning signs:**
- A "LOW" item mentions `throw`, `crash`, `data loss`, or `migration`.
- A "MEDIUM" item is in the app initialization path (`AppInitializer`, `main.dart`, `MigrationStrategy`).

**Phase to address:**
Audit phase (Phase 1). Severity classifications must be reviewed before work begins.

---

### Pitfall 15: SQLCipher / sqlite3 Symbol Conflict From Transitive Dependencies

**What goes wrong:**
`pubspec.yaml` correctly uses `sqlcipher_flutter_libs: ^0.6.7` without `sqlite3_flutter_libs`. The encrypted database executor in `lib/infrastructure/crypto/database/encrypted_database.dart` overrides the native library on Android via `open.overrideFor(OperatingSystem.android, openCipherOnAndroid)`. If any cleanup phase adds a new dependency (to support testing, linting, or documentation) whose transitive closure includes `sqlite3_flutter_libs`, the Android build fails at link time with duplicate native symbol errors. This is silent at the Dart level — `pubspec.yaml` resolves cleanly, `flutter pub get` succeeds — but the native build crashes.

**How to avoid:**
- Add a CI check: `grep sqlite3_flutter_libs pubspec.lock && echo "CONFLICT DETECTED" && exit 1`. Run this after every `flutter pub get` or `flutter pub upgrade`.
- When adding any dependency, explicitly check its `pubspec.yaml` for `sqlite3_flutter_libs` in its own dependencies.
- The `dependency_overrides` section is the fix if a transitive dep pulls it in: `sqlite3_flutter_libs: ">=0.0.0 <0.0.1"` to force an empty version.

**Warning signs:**
- `flutter build apk` fails with `duplicate symbol` or `multiple definition of sqlite3_*` linker errors.
- New dependency added and Android build breaks but iOS build is fine (SQLCipher conflict is Android-specific).

**Phase to address:**
Any phase that adds dependencies. Must be checked in the dependency health audit during the initial tooling setup.

---

## Technical Debt Patterns

| Shortcut | Immediate Benefit | Long-term Cost | When Acceptable |
|----------|-------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Committing `*.mocks.dart` instead of CI-generating | Simpler setup, no CI dependency | Stale mocks after every interface change; tests pass but verify wrong behavior | Never during a refactor initiative — migrate to CI generation or Mocktail |
| `// ignore: deprecated_member_use_from_same_package` on `ResolveLedgerTypeService` | Delay migration effort | Deprecated code stays in provider graph; new code copies the deprecated path | Acceptable as a short-term bridge only if tracked in the cleanup backlog |
| String interpolation in SQL migration steps (v14) | Faster to write | SQL injection vector if `DefaultCategories.all` ever contains a `'` character | Never — fix with parameterized statements before adding new categories |
| Static locale maps duplicating ARB content (`CategoryService`) | Dynamic key lookup impossible via `S` class | Translation drift — devs update one source and forget the other | Acceptable until a build-time consistency test guards the invariant |
| Hardcoded CJK strings in widget files (~169 occurrences) | Development speed | zh/en locales display Japanese; violates i18n rule | Never — must be extracted to ARB files |

---

## Integration Gotchas

| Integration | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|-------------|----------------|------------------|
| Drift `customIndices` | Using `Index()` constructor, omitting `#` Symbol prefix, adding `@override` annotation | `TableIndex(name: 'idx_table_col', columns: {#columnName})` — no override, no `@` prefix |
| Drift migrations | Adding a column or index without bumping `schemaVersion` + adding a migration step | Bump schema version, add `Migrator.createIndex` or `customStatement` step in `onUpgrade` |
| Drift migrations | Using string interpolation for SQL (`'${value}'`) in migration statements | Use parameterized statements: `customStatement('...', [value])` |
| SQLCipher init | Calling `appDatabaseProvider` before `KeyManager` initializes (key not yet derived) | Enforce `KeyManager → Database` ordering in `AppInitializer`; DB init depends on encryption key |
| Riverpod `@riverpod` refactor | Renaming provider function without regenerating `.g.dart` | Run `build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs` after every provider rename |
| `flutter gen-l10n` | Deleting a key from one ARB file and not the others | Edit all three ARB files atomically; regenerate immediately |
| Mockito `@GenerateMocks` | Changing a mocked interface method without regenerating `*.mocks.dart` | Run `build_runner build` after any interface change; treat committed mocks as requiring regeneration |

---

## Performance Traps

| Trap | Symptoms | Prevention | When It Breaks |
|------|----------|------------|----------------|
| `shrinkWrap: true` on nested `ReorderableListView` | Jank when expanding L1 categories with ≥30 L2 children (already present in `category_selection_screen.dart:493`) | Flatten to a single sliver list; do not introduce new `shrinkWrap: true` during widget refactors | Low-end Android with many categories |
| Building `AppDatabase.forTesting()` in `setUp` without `tearDown` `close()` | SQLite "too many open files" in large test suites | Always `await database.close()` in `tearDown` | Test suites with 50+ database tests |
| Full-table scan on `audit_logs` without timestamp index | Slow audit queries as log table grows unbounded | Add `TableIndex` on `timestamp` during the index-cleanup phase; must come with a schema bump | > 36k rows (~1 year of operation) |

---

## Security Mistakes

| Mistake | Risk | Prevention |
|---------|------|------------|
| Using `flutter_secure_storage` directly in new infrastructure code added during cleanup | Bypasses centralized key-storage boundary (verified clean in `CONCERNS.md`) | All secure storage access MUST go through `SecureStorageService` in `lib/infrastructure/security/` |
| Logging inside `recoverFromSeed()` after fixing the key-overwrite bug | Private key bytes or BIP39 words in debug logs | No `debugPrint` in key recovery paths; existing unguarded `debugPrint` sites in `sync_engine.dart` and `transaction_change_tracker.dart` must be wrapped in `if (kDebugMode)` |
| Copying the v14 migration string interpolation pattern | SQL injection if category names contain `'` | New migration steps must use parameterized `customStatement('... VALUES (?, ?)', [a, b])` |
| Adding `sqlite3_flutter_libs` transitively | Breaks SQLCipher encryption at link time; app may fall back to unencrypted SQLite | CI check on `pubspec.lock` for `sqlite3_flutter_libs` |

---

## "Looks Done But Isn't" Checklist

- [ ] **Provider deleted:** Verify the `.g.dart` part no longer references it — run `build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs` and check `flutter analyze` for 0 issues.
- [ ] **Deprecated service deleted:** Verify the provider, test file, and all `*.mocks.dart` that generated a `MockX` for the deleted type are updated. Run `flutter test`.
- [ ] **Table index added:** Verify `schemaVersion` was bumped and migration step exists. Write a test that opens a v(N-1) schema and upgrades to vN, then runs `PRAGMA index_list(table)`.
- [ ] **ARB key deleted:** Verify deletion from all three ARB files (`app_en.arb`, `app_ja.arb`, `app_zh.arb`), `flutter gen-l10n` runs cleanly, static locale maps in `CategoryService` do not reference the deleted key.
- [ ] **Provider consolidated:** Verify `keepAlive` status is preserved on the surviving definition. Verify no cross-feature layer violation was introduced.
- [ ] **`CategoryService` import touched:** Verify which of the two `CategoryService` classes is intended — `application/accounting/` (business logic) or `infrastructure/category/` (static locale map).
- [ ] **Coverage gate passed:** Verify every new test has at least one `expect()` or `verify()` call, not just line execution.
- [ ] **Build confirmed on all platforms:** `flutter build ios --no-codesign` and `flutter build apk` both pass — the SQLCipher conflict only surfaces in native builds.

---

## Recovery Strategies

| Pitfall | Recovery Cost | Recovery Steps |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Stale `.g.dart` causing runtime crash | LOW | `flutter pub run build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs && flutter test` |
| Stale `*.mocks.dart` causing silent behavior mismatch | MEDIUM | Identify which interfaces changed, run `build_runner build`, update test assertions that now fail |
| Deleted ARB key causing blank UI strings | LOW | Restore key to all three ARB files, `flutter gen-l10n`, verify |
| Drift schema without migration (index missing on device) | HIGH | Add a new migration step to the next schema version, bump `schemaVersion`, ship as a hotfix — cannot retroactively fix already-migrated devices without a follow-up migration |
| SQLCipher / sqlite3 symbol conflict | MEDIUM | Identify transitive dep, add `dependency_overrides`, `flutter pub get`, rebuild |
| `keepAlive` dropped from provider consolidation | HIGH | Restore `keepAlive: true`, run `build_runner build`, manual test session: navigate away and back while observing sync/state |
| Two `CategoryService` classes used incorrectly after import fix | MEDIUM | Revert the import, rename one class (e.g., `CategoryLocaleService`), update all sites |

---

## Pitfall-to-Phase Mapping

| Pitfall | Prevention Phase | Verification |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| Stale generated files (`.g.dart`, `.freezed.dart`) | All phases — enforce via CI gate | `build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs && git diff --exit-code lib/` |
| Committed `*.mocks.dart` stale after interface change | Dead-code + interface refactor phases | Run `flutter test` after `build_runner build`; all mocks consistent |
| Dual `CategoryService` class collision | Redundant-code phase (rename one) | `grep -r "category_service.dart" lib/` returns unambiguous single import paths |
| `appDatabaseProvider` override missing in tests | Riverpod hygiene phase | Add shared `createTestProviderScope` helper that includes override by default |
| Drift schema without migration | Any phase touching `lib/data/tables/` | Schema-upgrade integration test: v(N-1)→vN migration with schema assertions |
| Deprecated service deletion (cascade errors) | Dead-code phase | Deletion checklist: source, provider, `.g.dart`, test, mocks — each verified clean |
| ARB key deletion without static map audit | Dead-code / i18n cleanup phase | Test: `CategoryService.getName(id, 'zh')` returns non-empty for all category IDs |
| `autoDispose` / `keepAlive` lifecycle broken by consolidation | Riverpod hygiene phase | Lifecycle tests: dispose all listeners, assert provider state matches expected `keepAlive` |
| `ref.read` vs `ref.watch` confusion | Riverpod hygiene phase | `riverpod_lint` enabled; `flutter analyze` 0 issues after every provider change |
| `FontFeature.tabularFigures()` dropped in widget refactor | Theme cleanup / dead-code phase | Widget test asserting `fontFeatures` present on monetary `Text` widgets |
| Static locale map entries flagged as dead by AI scanner | Dead-code phase (AI agent scan) | Explicit allowlist in agent prompt: static map entries in `CategoryService` are live |
| Provider consolidation changes scope/crosses layers | Riverpod hygiene phase | `flutter analyze` with layer-violation lint; manual trace of each provider's consumers |
| Coverage gaming (tests without assertions) | All phases | PR review gate: every `test(...)` block must contain `expect()` or `verify()` |
| Severity misclassification | Audit phase | Re-review severity of any item touching init path, data integrity, or migrations |
| SQLCipher / sqlite3 conflict from transitive dep | Dependency health audit (tooling setup) | CI: `grep sqlite3_flutter_libs pubspec.lock && exit 1` |

---

## CLAUDE.md Existing 13 Pitfalls — Coverage Review

All 13 pitfalls from `CLAUDE.md` are incorporated or addressed:

| CLAUDE.md Pitfall | Addressed In |
|-------------------|--------------|
| 1. Don't modify generated files (`.g.dart`, `.freezed.dart`) | Pitfall 1 (stale gen files) |
| 2. Don't violate layer dependencies | Pitfall 12 (provider scope/layer violation) |
| 3. Don't skip code generation after modifying annotated classes | Pitfall 1 (stale gen files) — primary coverage |
| 4. Don't mutate objects — always use `copyWith` | Not a refactor-specific risk; covered by project coding-style rules |
| 5. Don't use `intl` version other than 0.20.2 | Fragile areas — `CONCERNS.md` covers this; not a refactor pitfall per se |
| 6. Don't add `sqlite3_flutter_libs` | Pitfall 15 (SQLCipher conflict) |
| 7. Don't modify Podfile `post_install` without preserving EXCLUDED_ARCHS fix | Out of scope for this cleanup (no ML Kit being added) |
| 8. Don't commit with analyzer warnings | Quality gate throughout; Pitfall 1 enforcement |
| 9. Don't hardcode widget parameter defaults — use nullable + provider fallback | Not a refactor risk specific to this initiative |
| 10. Don't duplicate repository provider definitions | Pitfall 12 (provider duplication / scope) |
| 11. Don't use wrong Drift index syntax — use `TableIndex` with `{#column}` | Integration Gotchas table |
| 12. Don't skip AppInitializer — initialize core services before `runApp()` | Pitfall 4 (appDatabaseProvider override) + Pitfall 5 (SQLCipher init ordering) |
| 13. Don't forget to regenerate code after merge/pull | Pitfall 1 (stale gen files) |

---

## Sources

- `.planning/codebase/CONCERNS.md` — direct codebase audit findings (2026-04-25)
- `.planning/codebase/TESTING.md` — test infrastructure analysis
- `.planning/PROJECT.md` — project constraints and scope
- `CLAUDE.md` — project-specific pitfall list (13 items, all reviewed above)
- Direct file inspection: `lib/data/app_database.dart` (migration SQL), `lib/infrastructure/security/providers.dart` (UnimplementedError pattern), `lib/infrastructure/category/category_service.dart` (dual-class naming), `lib/features/accounting/presentation/providers/use_case_providers.dart` (deprecated service usage), `lib/l10n/app_*.arb` (key count divergence), `analysis_options.yaml` (generated file exclusions)
- Riverpod 2.4+ documentation: `keepAlive` lifecycle, `autoDispose` behavior, `ref.read` vs `ref.watch` semantics
- Drift documentation: `TableIndex` syntax, `MigrationStrategy.onUpgrade`, parameterized `customStatement`
